S (~250 LoC + tests).

## Synergy

Most of the data already exists somewhere:
- VERSION opcode (chevron daemon version path) — already done
- Schema check (history.rs check_schema) — exists
- src/health/checks.rs — exists, just needs user-facing exposure
- daemon paths (src/daemon/paths.rs) — exists

This issue is mostly composition. Implementing it is one new src/doctor.rs and a CLI subcommand.

## Reference

atuin's 'atuin doctor' + 'atuin info' are the model. See https://docs.atuin.sh/cli/reference/doctor/.

title: Recording-side filters: history_filter, cwd_filter, store_failed
description:
Three write-time exclusion controls. Drop matching commands at CmdEnd time so they never enter the commands table. Distinct from query-time --exclude flags (which filter at read time but the rows are still recorded).

## Why this matters

Users want "never record commands matching these patterns" rules. Leading-space (HISTCONTROL=ignorespace) is too fragile — it requires per-invocation discipline. A persistent rule that says 'never record aws sso login *' or 'never record anything from ~/Downloads' is the canonical fix. atuin pairs this with history prune --dry-run for retroactive cleanup of pre-rule entries.

## Config surface (new [history] section)

[history]
history_filter = ["^aws sso login", "^secret-tool"]  # regexes; cmd matching ANY is dropped
cwd_filter = ["~/Downloads/.*", ".*/tmp$"]  # regexes; cwd matching ANY drops the entry
store_failed = true  # default true (matches today); false drops non-zero exits

## Acceptance criteria

- A command matching a history_filter regex doesn't appear in 'chevron history'
- A command run in a cwd matching cwd_filter doesn't appear
- store_failed = false drops rows where exit_status != 0 (kept silent: NULL exits — still-running — are kept until they finish)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture filter configs

## Effort

S+S+XS combined, total ~250 LoC + tests. File as one issue because all three share insertion point (chevrond's CmdEnd / state.rs).

## Synergy with chevron-ckz

Secret detection (chevron-ckz, filed) is the third leg of the same stool: instead of regex rules, ckz inspects content for known secret patterns. Likely shares plumbing once both land.

## Out of scope

- Per-shell or per-session filter rules. Global config only for v1.
- Allow-lists (the inverse: 'only record commands matching X'). Drop into 'out of scope unless requested.'

title: Frecency scoring for command ranking
description:
Rank search results by a configurable combination of frequency (how often run) and recency (how recently). atuin's frecency_score_multiplier / frequency_score_multiplier / recency_score_multiplier model.

## Why this matters

Users notice "the right command at the top" more than they notice fuzzy vs prefix matching. atuin's frecency-default ranking is what makes its TUI feel smart. Without this, chevron's TUI ships with chronological-default ranking which works but feels dumber.

## Surface

- New 'rank' column on commands table (or computed on-the-fly initially; could become persistent later)
- New [search.ranking] config section:
  - frequency_weight (default: 1.0)
  - recency_weight (default: 1.0)
  - frecency_weight (default: 0.0 — opt-in to combined frecency)
- 'chevron history --rank frecency|recent|alpha|duration' to override
- TUI uses the configured default

## Acceptance criteria

- A heavily-used command ('git status') outranks a rarely-used one in default frecency view
- A recently-used command outranks an old one with equal frequency
- 'chevron history --rank alpha' returns alphabetically-sorted results (current behaviour is started_at-DESC; this changes default to frecency only when configured)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture commands of varying frequency + recency

## Effort

M (~300 LoC + tests).

## Sequence

Best after the TUI lands (chevron-1yq.1.1). Ranking is invisible without an interactive consumer that surfaces it.

## Synergy

The actor's existing CmdEnd handler is the place to increment a 'last_run / run_count' pair on the matching row (or recompute frecency on every render — pick based on benchmarks).

## Out of scope

- Per-cwd frecency (rank by 'how often used IN THIS CWD'). Useful but distinct algorithm.
- Hand-tunable scoring formulas beyond multipliers.

title: History lifecycle commands: delete --query, dedup, prune
description:
Three commands that let users clean up the commands table after recording. Today chevron has no write path from the CLI — chevron history is strictly read-only. This issue introduces controlled writes.

## Surface

- 'chevron history delete --query <filter-expr>' — delete rows matching the same filter syntax chevron history uses (--cwd, --grep, --since, etc.)
- 'chevron history dedup --dry-run --keep N' — collapse exact-duplicate commands, keeping the most-recent N copies per cmd
- 'chevron history prune --dry-run' — delete rows that NOW match the configured history_filter / cwd_filter (retroactive cleanup after the user adds new filter rules; sibling to chevron-1yq.2.1)
- All three default to --dry-run; require explicit --yes (or --no-dry-run) to actually delete

## Wire / daemon

New CMD_DELETE / CMD_PRUNE opcodes on chevrond. Owner of writes stays the daemon (CLI read-only invariant preserved). Same shape as Phase 1's CMD_END.

## Acceptance criteria

- 'delete --query --cwd /tmp --yes' deletes all rows where cwd = '/tmp' (none from other cwds)
- 'dedup --keep 1 --yes' collapses identical cmd values into one row each (most-recent kept)
- 'prune --yes' deletes rows matching the configured history_filter regexes
- Output capture files (outputs/<id>.log) are deleted alongside their command rows
- All three reject without --yes when --dry-run is false (default --dry-run prevents accidents)

## Effort

M total (~400 LoC + tests). One of the larger leaves — touches daemon protocol AND history CLI AND a new ownership model.

## Depends on

chevron-1yq.2.1 (recording-side filters) for the prune subcommand specifically. Delete and dedup are independent and could ship first.

## Synergy with sync (chevron-1yr)

When sync lands, delete becomes a soft-delete (tombstone record) that syncs to other machines. Don't try to support that here — v1 is hard-delete only. Sync deletion is its own design.

title: Operations & UX polish: chevron doctor, CLI ergonomics, systemd
description:
Three smaller items: diagnostic subcommand for bug reports, ergonomic flags missing from `chevron history`, and systemd socket activation for the daemon.

## Children

- chevron doctor diagnostic subcommand — S, P3 — promote internal health/ to user-facing
- History CLI ergonomic omnibus (--print0, --offset, etc.) — XS, P4 — small CLI gaps
- Systemd socket activation for chevrond — S, P4 — Linux-server users

## Why this matters

- doctor: when a user files a bug, they need to dump version/config/paths/schema-version. Atuin has `atuin doctor` and `atuin info`. Chevron has `health` but it's not a user-facing subcommand for "give me the support bundle."
- CLI ergonomics: small things like --print0 (null-separated output for xargs -0) that block scripted composition.
- systemd: serious Linux users running long-lived sessions want socket activation, not "spawn-on-cache-miss."

None of these are headline features. All three are P3-P4 papercut closers that the next user to file a bug will appreciate.

title: Recording lifecycle: write-time filters, delete/dedup/prune
description:
Atuin parity for the "control what gets recorded and clean up what shouldn't have been" surface. Two leaves:

## Children

- Recording-side filters (history_filter, cwd_filter, store_failed) — S each, P3 — file together since they share the same insertion point in chevrond's CmdEnd handler
- History lifecycle commands (delete --query, dedup, prune) — M/S/S combined, P3 — share write-from-daemon path

## Why this matters

Today chevron has no way to say "never record `aws sso login *`" (a leading-space workaround on every invocation is fragile). And once you decide "I should have been excluding these," there's no way to retroactively clean up — the history grows monotonically.

Atuin pairs `history_filter` + `prune --dry-run` as the canonical "set a rule + clean up retroactively" workflow.

## Synergy

Both leaves touch the daemon's write path. Currently chevron has no write-from-history-CLI path at all (chevron history opens the DB read-only). This sub-epic introduces it. New socket opcodes (CMD_DELETE, CMD_PRUNE) so the daemon owns writes — the read-only-DB invariant of chevron history is preserved.

## Out of scope for this sub-epic

- Secret detection / redaction (filed: chevron-ckz) — sibling lifecycle concern but different mechanism
- Retention / LRU eviction of capture FILES (filed: chevron-rzr)

title: Memory / load / disk segments
description:
Three small segments sharing the same shape: read system stat, format, display. Share a daemon-cache extension (StateMsg::GetSystemStats with 5s TTL).

## Segments

- Memory: free/used/total. macOS: vm_stat. Linux: /proc/meminfo. Already done in src/health/checks.rs.
- Load: 1-minute load average via libc getloadavg(). Already in health/.
- Disk: free space at cwd (or configurable mountpoint). macOS/Linux: statvfs.

## Surface

- Three segments: memory, load, disk
- Each with thresholds (e.g. red >90%, yellow >70%, green <=70%) + display format
- All off by default

## Acceptance criteria

- Each segment renders correct values on macOS and Linux
- Snapshot-tested with fixture system stats (mock the syscalls)
- Cached for ~5s via daemon (otherwise prompt-render syscalls add latency on cold render)

## Effort

M (~400 LoC including the daemon-cache extension and tests for all three).

## Synergy

chevrond extends with a "system stats" cache entry. New StateMsg::GetSystemStats with a similar shape to the existing RepoStatus get. The 5s TTL is enough — kernel load average updates that infrequently anyway.

## Why combined for the leaf

All three share: same syscall pattern, same daemon-cache shape, same threshold/format config. Shipping as one PR keeps the daemon-cache extension focused.

## Out of scope

- Per-process resource use (gnome-system-monitor territory)
- CPU temperature (platform-specific, defer)
- Network throughput

title: Battery segment
description:
Display battery percentage + charging indicator icon. macOS: parse 'pmset -g batt'. Linux: read /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/{capacity,status}.

## Surface

- Segment name: battery
- Default icon: Nerd Font battery glyph that varies by charge level (0/25/50/75/100 + charging variant)
- Config: thresholds for color change (e.g. red <15%, yellow <30%, green >30%)
- Hidden on desktops (no battery present → segment renders nothing)

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders percentage + icon on a laptop with a detected battery
- Renders nothing on a desktop (graceful when /sys/class/power_supply is empty or pmset reports no battery)
- Renders nothing if the segment isn't configured (off by default)
- Snapshot-tested with mocked battery state fixtures

## Effort

S (~150 LoC + tests).

## Synergy

src/health/ may already have battery detection logic for the health subcommand — check before duplicating. If not, this segment can be reused by health/ later.

## Out of scope

- Time-remaining estimate (varies per platform, complex)
- Per-cell health

title: Jujutsu (jj) segment
description:
Detect a jj repo (.jj/ dir in cwd or ancestor) and render the current change description + bookmark info via 'jj log -r @ -T <template>'.

## Why this matters

jj is gaining momentum in the developer audience that overlaps heavily with chevron's target demographic (prompt-customisation enthusiasts, Atuin users, etc.). Starship has it; chevron doesn't. Closing this is small-effort + visible parity win.

## Surface

- Segment name: jj
- Default format: change-id short prefix + bookmark name if any (e.g. 'qpvuntsm main')
- Daemon-cached results for ~5s (jj log is slow on cold runs)
- FS-watch invalidation on .jj/ changes

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders nothing outside a jj repo (no .jj/ in any ancestor)
- Renders change-id + bookmark inside a jj repo
- Daemon caches results per workdir using the same pattern as RepoStatus
- FS-watch invalidation when .jj/ changes
- Snapshot-tested with fixture jj repos

## Effort

M (~300 LoC including daemon-cache extension).

## Synergy with chevrond

Same shape as the existing git-status caching in src/daemon/state.rs's State struct. The cached entry is keyed by jj-workdir, populated on demand, invalidated by FS watch. ~80% of the code is mirror-existing-code.

## Out of scope for v1

- jj operation indicators (rebase/squash/conflict). v1 just shows current change + bookmark.
- Co-located git+jj repos (a single segment showing jj OR git, not both)

## Reference

jj's CLI: 'jj log -r @ -T 'change_id.short() ++ " " ++ bookmarks''' returns the data we need in one subprocess invocation.

title: Themes / presets bundled with chevron init --preset
description:
Ship 4-6 example config.toml files under presets/ (lean, rainbow, classic, pure-like, minimal, two-line). New CLI: 'chevron init --preset rainbow' writes the corresponding TOML to the user's config path.

## Why this matters

A user trying chevron has no example to copy from. Pure presets are responsible for a lot of Starship's first-impression: 'chevron init --preset rainbow' should produce a visually distinctive prompt with no further work.

## Surface

- 'chevron init --preset <name>' writes the preset's config to \$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/chevron/config.toml (or CHEVRON_CONFIG override)
- 'chevron init --list-presets' shows available presets with one-line descriptions
- Presets stored as static const &str values in src/presets.rs or similar (no FS read at runtime)

## Presets to ship

- default: minimal, similar to current chevron defaults
- rainbow: powerline-flavoured with colours per segment (similar to Starship's rainbow preset)
- lean: single-line, no powerline arrows
- two-line: cwd on line 1, prompt-char on line 2 (Pure-style)
- minimal: just user@host + cwd + git
- pure-like: emulate Sindre's Pure theme

## Acceptance criteria

- All presets included as compile-time strings (no runtime FS dependency)
- Each preset visually distinct (snapshot-tested by rendering each in a fixture cwd + git state)
- 'chevron init --list-presets' shows the list
- Documentation: README has a section with screenshots of each preset

## Effort

S (~150 LoC + the preset TOML files themselves).

## Synergy

Complements chevron-1yp.3.4 (configuration wizard) — the wizard can choose a preset as a starting point and let the user tweak from there.

title: Declarative detect_files / detect_folders / detect_extensions in TOML
description:
Lift the hard-coded ancestor-file-detection logic from each language segment into TOML config. Currently node.rs has 'find_ancestor("package.json", 5)' baked in; this issue makes it 'detect_files = ["package.json", "package-lock.json"]' in config.

## Why this matters

Users adding a new language segment via TOML alone (no chevron rebuild) is the Starship convention. Power users have config repos they share; this enables that.

## Surface

- New detect_files, detect_folders, detect_extensions arrays in per-segment config
- Defaults preserve current behaviour (built-in language segments have correct defaults so a fresh install behaves identically to today)
- Users can add new file triggers without a chevron rebuild

## Acceptance criteria

- A user adds 'detect_extensions = [".scala"]' to a user-defined Scala-language segment in their config and the segment renders for *.scala-containing trees
- Built-in segments have correct defaults
- Snapshot-tested with custom detect_* config
- Documentation: example TOML showing detect_files/detect_folders/detect_extensions in README

## Depends on

chevron-1yp.3.1 (format-string per segment refactor). The same Segment trait changes that enable templated format strings also enable templated detection rules.

## Effort

S after 3.1 lands (~150 LoC).

title: show_on_command conditional segment visibility
description:
p10k feature: segments declare themselves visible only when a certain command is about to run. Example: AWS segment shows when typing 'aws s3 ls' but is hidden for 'ls /tmp'.

## Why this matters

Power users build prompts with many segments. Conditional visibility means "show me the k8s context only when I'm about to run a k8s command" — reduces visual noise.

## Surface

- New show_on_command array in per-segment config: 'show_on_command = ["kubectl", "kustomize", "helm"]'
- preexec stashes the about-to-run command's first token in CHEVRON_CMD_FIRST_TOKEN env var
- Segments check whether their show_on_command list contains the current command's first token; hide if not (default: always show)

## Acceptance criteria

- A kubectl segment with show_on_command=["kubectl"] renders only when the user is about to type 'kubectl' (or 'kubectl get pods' etc.)
- All segments without show_on_command continue to render unconditionally
- The preexec stash uses the existing _chevron_cmd_id machinery (chevron-1yn.1)
- Snapshot tests verify both rendered-and-hidden states

## Effort

M (~250 LoC + shell init changes + tests).

## Synergy

chevron-1yn.1 already stashes the about-to-run command in \$1 of preexec. This issue exposes it as an env var the segments can read. Daemon doesn't need to be involved.

## Reference

p10k's source: see the show_on_command field in p10k.zsh.

title: OS icon segment
description:
Tiny segment, popular look. Detect the OS from /etc/os-release (Linux) or uname (macOS/BSD), display a Nerd Font glyph: Apple logo for macOS, distro-specific logo for Linux (Arch, Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora, NixOS, etc.), BSD daemon for BSDs.

## Surface

- Segment name: os
- Config: icon_set (glyph map by distro id) + format override
- Default behaviour: detect-and-display; user can disable via config

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders the Apple logo on macOS
- Renders distro-specific logo on Linux when /etc/os-release has ID=arch | debian | ubuntu | fedora | nixos | gentoo | manjaro | opensuse | rhel | centos
- Renders a generic Linux penguin glyph for unknown distros
- Renders the BSD daemon for OpenBSD/FreeBSD/NetBSD
- Snapshot-tested with fixture os-release files

## Effort

S (~80 LoC + tests).

## Reference

src/segments/nix_shell.rs is the closest template — also a 'detect-and-display-icon' segment with simple conditional rendering.

## Out of scope

- Architecture sub-display (x86_64, aarch64, etc.)
- Kernel version

title: .NET runtime segment
description:
Detect .NET via ancestor lookup for *.csproj, *.fsproj, *.vbproj, *.sln, global.json. Display the SDK version from global.json's 'sdk.version' field, or invoke 'dotnet --version' as last resort.

## Surface

- Segment name: dotnet
- Default icon: Nerd Font .NET glyph
- Default format: '{ICON} {version}'
- Detection precedence: global.json sdk.version → dotnet --version → skip

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders nothing without a .NET ancestor
- Prefers global.json (fast, no subprocess) over invoking dotnet
- Reports the SDK version (not the runtime version)
- Falls back to 'dotnet --version' subprocess only when global.json is absent
- Snapshot-tested

## Out of scope

- TargetFramework display from *.csproj (parsing XML for that is overkill for v1).

## Effort

S (~150 LoC + tests).

title: Java runtime segment
description:
Detect Java via ancestor lookup for pom.xml, build.gradle, build.gradle.kts, *.java, *.kt. Display the version from .java-version, JAVA_HOME (parse directory name like 'jdk-17.0.5'), or .tool-versions java line.

## Surface

- Segment name: java
- Default icon: Nerd Font Java glyph
- Default format: '{ICON} {version}'
- Detection precedence: .java-version → .tool-versions java line → JAVA_HOME dir name → skip

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders nothing without a Java ancestor
- Renders the version from .java-version when present
- Parses 'jdk-17.0.5' or 'openjdk-17' style names from JAVA_HOME
- Snapshot-tested

## Out of scope

- Invoking 'java -version' (~500ms cold-start latency — too slow for a prompt render path). Documented limitation: a pure-env-var user without a version file gets no segment.
- Maven/Gradle build version display.

## Effort

S (~150 LoC + tests).

## Synergy

Same as Ruby: chevron-1yp.5.1 (.tool-versions) covers this for users who use asdf/mise.

title: VCS expansion: Jujutsu (jj) support
description:
Jujutsu (jj) is gaining momentum among the developer audience that overlaps heavily with chevron's "shell-aware session brain" demographic. Starship has it; chevron doesn't. hg/fossil/pijul are too niche to prioritise — file as user-request issues if asked.

## Children

- chevron-1yp.5.1: Jujutsu (jj) segment

## Why a sub-epic for one child

Leaves room to add hg/fossil/pijul under this epic later if users ask, without restructuring. If we never add them, this epic just has the one child — fine.

## Synergy

The chevrond state actor already has a perfect cache-and-watch shape for git via state.rs's RepoStatus cache. Adding jj is the same pattern: keyed by jj-workdir, populated on demand, FS-watch invalidation on .jj/ changes. ~300 LoC of mostly-mirror-existing-code.

title: System resource segments (battery, memory, load, disk)
description:
Battery, memory, load average, disk usage — staple segments in Starship and p10k. chevron's health/ subsystem (chevron health subcommand) already computes most of these for its health checks; this epic exposes them as prompt segments with daemon-cached results.

## Children

- chevron-1yp.4.1: Battery segment (S)
- chevron-1yp.4.2: Memory / load / disk segments (M; combined since they share daemon-cache shape)

## Synergy

chevrond extends with a "system stats" cache entry (StateMsg::GetSystemStats) using the same pattern as the existing RepoStatus cache. 5s TTL is enough — battery doesn't change minute-to-minute, load average is updated by the kernel every 5s anyway. Adds one syscall per refresh window.

## Out of scope for v1

- CPU temperature / frequency
- Per-process resource use
- Network throughput
- Public IP (deferred as separate request if users ask)

## Visibility default

OFF by default. Users explicitly opt-in via config — system segments are matter-of-taste and clutter the prompt unless wanted.

title: Phase 4.1: per-cwd auto-capture via accept-line widget
description: The policy layer on top of chevron-1yn.4's PTY wrapper. zsh init defines an accept-line widget that rewrites the command buffer to prepend 'chevron capture' when (a) CHEVRON_CAPTURE_HERE=1 is set, or (b) cwd matches a config-file glob. Must respect an interactive-program blocklist (vim/less/ssh/sudo/htop/python/node/bash/zsh/fish + shell builtins) so we don't insert a PTY layer between the terminal and vim's own PTY handling. Mode 2 of the original Phase 4 design; mode 1 (explicit chcap) shipped in chevron-1yn.4.

title: Phase 4.2: sliding-window output capture
description: V1 keeps the FIRST N MB of captured output and marks output_truncated=1 when overflowing. Build errors usually fall at the END so the more useful behavior is sliding-window (keep the LAST N MB). Two implementation approaches: (a) ring file with mmap (fixed size, wraps around — efficient but file format becomes non-linear), (b) write-to-temp + truncate-from-front periodically (simple but writes are amplified). Both have edge cases (signal during truncate, partial writes mid-ring) — evaluate against the simpler v1 behavior before committing.

title: Phase 4.3: aggregate retention / LRU eviction of capture files
description: Per-file cap is 10MB (configurable). Total disk usage grows unbounded as the user accumulates captures. Add a retention policy: e.g. delete output files for commands older than N days, or evict LRU files when total outputs/ size exceeds M MB. Daemon-side responsibility; runs at startup + periodically.

title: Phase 4.5: secret detection / redaction in captured output
description: Captured output may contain tokens, API keys, passwords printed accidentally. Per-cwd opt-in is the first line of defense; the second is detecting common patterns (AWS keys, Slack tokens, JWTs, raw private keys) at capture time and redacting them. Detector lib options: trufflehog regex set, gitleaks rules, or a small hand-rolled set targeted at command-output noise (different shape than git-blob secrets). Evaluate false-positive rate vs the safety win.

title: Phase 3.3: fish live prompt support
description: Fish has commandline -f repaint which is the moral equivalent of zle reset-prompt. The fish_prompt function reruns, the prompt redraws. Should be a small port of the zsh wiring from chevron-1yn.3 — wire a background fd to a fish event handler. Less risk than bash.

title: Phase 3.1: worktree watch for working-tree-edit liveness
description: Phase 3 inherits Phase 1's gitdir-only FS watch scope. Vim saves, build output, untracked-file additions don't trigger an EVENT — they rely on the 100ms TTL. Adding a recursive worktree watch alongside the existing gitdir watch would close the gap. Hard part: avoiding inotify-watch exhaustion on big trees like node_modules/ + target/. Need to use git's gitignore data to skip ignored directories from the watch set. notify supports custom filters; libgit2 has check_ignore. Combine the two.

title: Phase 3.4: chevron subscribe auto-reconnect
description: Currently when the daemon restarts (chevron daemon stop / start or crash), the subscriber's read end gets EOF and the helper exits cleanly. The shell's zle -F callback notices via the unregister-on-EOF path and the user loses live updates until they open a new shell. Add a reconnect loop with backoff: on EOF, sleep + retry the connection up to N times. Bounded so a permanently-dead daemon doesn't pin the helper.

title: Phase 2.4: fuzzy and prefix search modes for chevron history
description: Atuin's --search-mode flag: prefix | fulltext | fuzzy | skim. Currently chevron history --grep does substring match only (LIKE '%pat%'). Adding fuzzy needs a fuzzy-matcher (probably skim-matcher or fuzzy-matcher crate). Prefix is trivial (LIKE 'pat%'). Useful for shell key bindings that integrate with fzf.

title: Phase 2.6: canonicalize cwd at write time for symlink-stable --workspace
description: Phase 1 stores cwd as whatever the shell hands the preexec hook — typically $PWD with symlinks intact. Phase 2 --workspace uses libgit2 to find the work-tree root, which is canonicalized. Symlinked navigation can cause stored cwds and workspace-root to not match. Fix: canonicalize at write time (chevron event cmd-start). Small Phase 1 amendment; needs migration handling for existing rows ('SET cwd = canon(cwd)' one-shot, or accept that old rows have non-canonical paths).

title: Phase 2.5: export CHEVRON_SESSION_ID from shell init
description: Phase 2 of chevron-1yn ships --filter-mode but session isn't supported because the shell init scripts set _chevron_session_id as a private shell-local, not exported. Tiny amendment: also 'export CHEVRON_SESSION_ID=...' alongside, so 'chevron history --this-session' / '--filter-mode session' work without explicit --session <id>. Touches src/shell.rs (zsh+bash+fish init), tests follow existing init-script pattern.

title: Phase 2.2: chevron history import bash|zsh|fish
description: Import existing shell history files into the chevrond commands log. Atuin parity (atuin import). One-time bootstrap so users see their existing history immediately after enabling chevron. Should reuse the same wire protocol (CMD_START + CMD_END) so events flow through chevrond exactly like live commands.

title: Phase 2.3: calendar-aware time parsing for --since/--until
description: Support 'today', 'yesterday', day-of-week names ('monday'), 'last week', and absolute dates ('2026-05-22'). Needs a date library — chrono or jiff most likely. v1 (chevron-1yn.2) supports compact (1d/30m) + 'N units ago' + 'now' + --since-ms escape hatch. This issue adds the rest.

title: Phase 2.1: chevron history stats subcommand
description: Aggregate stats from the commands log: top-N by frequency, by cwd, by exit status, count over time. Atuin parity (atuin stats). Depends on chevron-1yn.2 (the history CLI infrastructure).

title: History CLI ergonomic omnibus: --print0, --offset, etc.
description:
Bundle of small ergonomic gaps in 'chevron history' that don't deserve their own issue. atuin parity for shell-composition flags.

## Surface additions

- --print0: null-separated output (for 'chevron history -G error -f cmds --print0 | xargs -0 ...')
- --offset N: skip first N results (pagination — currently no way to page through results larger than --limit)
- --reverse-output: like --reverse but applied AFTER sort (subtle but real difference)
- --hostname-include / --hostname-exclude with glob support (currently --host is exact-match only)
- --print-headers: header row on tabular output (off by default)

## Acceptance criteria

- 'chevron history --print0 -f cmds' emits null-separated lines
- 'chevron history --limit 25 --offset 25' returns the second page
- Hostname glob 'work-*' matches 'work-laptop' and 'work-desktop'
- Snapshot-tested for each new flag

## Effort

XS (~150 LoC + tests). File as one omnibus issue rather than 5 separate XS issues.

## Synergy

All five are local additions to src/history.rs's existing arg-parsing block (around the existing --limit / --reverse handling). No daemon, no schema changes.

title: Systemd socket activation for chevrond
description:
Linux server / persistent-shell users expect chevrond to integrate with systemd: socket activation, status reporting via sd_notify, and a packaged unit file.

## Why this matters

On a long-lived dev box, users want chevrond to start on-demand from socket activation rather than being spawn-on-cache-miss from a shell. systemd is the standard mechanism. atuin has '[daemon] systemd_socket = true' for this.

## Surface

- New flag 'chevron daemon serve --systemd' (or auto-detect via LISTEN_FDS env)
- When invoked under systemd socket activation:
  - chevrond uses sd_listen_fds(3) to receive the pre-opened socket from systemd
  - Doesn't try to bind a new socket
  - Calls sd_notify(READY=1) when accept loop is running
  - Calls sd_notify(STOPPING=1) on shutdown
- Ship a chevrond.socket + chevrond.service unit file in dist/systemd/ (or under the existing presets/)

## Acceptance criteria

- 'systemctl --user start chevrond.socket' activates the daemon on first connection
- 'systemctl --user status chevrond' shows running once a shell connects
- Sigterm via 'systemctl --user stop chevrond' gracefully shuts down
- The non-systemd path (chevron daemon start) still works unchanged on macOS and non-systemd Linux

## Effort

S (~200 LoC + unit file + integration test on Linux).

## Reference

systemd's sd_listen_fds(3) man page. The libsystemd C lib is annoying to link from Rust; use the 'sd-notify' crate (~30KB, pure Rust, MIT-licensed).

## Out of scope

- launchd plist for macOS (file as P4 follow-up if requested).
- Socket activation under runit / openrc / other init systems.

title: Daemon-side hot fuzzy index for sub-ms search
description:
Keep an in-memory fuzzy-search index of all command rows inside chevrond. Sub-millisecond results vs ~10ms cold SQLite scans.

## Why this matters

When the TUI is interactive (users type to filter), every keystroke triggers a search. SQLite LIKE/GLOB is fast for a few thousand rows but degrades for power users with 100k+ rows of history. atuin's daemon-fuzzy mode keeps a hot index so search stays sub-ms regardless of history size.

## Surface

- chevrond's state actor maintains a fuzzy-search-ready in-memory structure (probably a SkimMatcher or similar) populated at startup from the commands table
- On CmdStart/CmdEnd, the actor updates the index incrementally
- 'chevron search' (and 'chevron history --rank fuzzy') route through the index instead of SQL when the daemon is available
- Fallback: when the daemon is down, search falls back to SQL LIKE (same as today)

## Acceptance criteria

- A search across 100k+ rows of fixture history returns in <2ms via the daemon (measured)
- The same search via the SQL fallback path is correct but slower (acceptable degradation)
- Memory footprint stays under ~50MB for 100k rows

## Effort

M (~400 LoC + tests + benchmark).

## Sequence

P4. Defer until the TUI ships AND someone (probably you) has hit the perf wall. Premature optimization without an interactive caller.

## Reference

atuin's daemon-fuzzy mode in https://github.com/atuinsh/atuin (search the daemon code for the in-memory index logic). The fuzzy_matcher crate is the popular Rust choice.

## Out of scope

- Persistent on-disk fuzzy index. The in-memory rebuild cost at daemon startup is acceptable for <500k rows (~100ms one-time hit).
- Server-side fuzzy index for synced records (Phase 8+ when sync exists).

title: Phase 4.4: FTS5 index for output search
description: chevron history --grep-output currently does a linear scan of candidate output files (post-SQL). For a power user with thousands of captured commands, this gets slow. SQLite FTS5 indexed against captured output would make 'find the build where I saw that error' near-instant. Trade-off: ~30% disk overhead for the index, write-time cost on capture. Worth the cost once the user base has captures in the thousands.

title: Phase 3.2: bash live prompt support
description: Phase 3 (chevron-1yn.3) ships zsh only because zle reset-prompt has no clean bash equivalent. The two viable workarounds are: (a) bind -x + a key sequence that the daemon SIGURGs the shell to inject, (b) PROMPT_COMMAND-based polling at fixed cadence. Both have rough edges with multi-line input, autosuggestions, and other tools that wrap PS1. Investigate which (if either) lands within acceptable behavior.

title: Phase 3.5: topic filtering at the daemon
description: Currently subscribers see both 'git' and 'cmd' topics. The wire format already has type= field so subscribers can filter client-side. Adding a topic-filter field to SUBSCRIBE (topics=git,cmd or topics=git) would cut bytes on the wire when a consumer only wants one. Implement when a use case actually needs it; the v1 zsh init wants both and per-cwd filter is sufficient.
